content.problemStatement:
Chef and N−1N-1N−1 more of his friends go to the night canteen. The canteen serves only three items (well, they serve more, but only these three are edible!), which are omelette, chocolate milkshake, and chocolate cake. Their prices are AAA, BBB and CCC respectively.

However, the canteen is about to run out of some ingredients. In particular, they only have EEE eggs and HHH chocolate bars left. They need:


222 eggs to make an omelette
333 chocolate bars for a chocolate milkshake
111 egg and 111 chocolate bar for a chocolate cake
Each of the NNN friends wants to order one item. They can only place an order if the canteen has enough ingredients to prepare all the ordered items. Find the smallest possible total price they have to pay or determine that it is impossible to prepare NNN items.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains six space-separated integers NNN, EEE, HHH, AAA, BBB and CCC.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the minimum cost of NNN items, or −1-1−1 if it is impossible to prepare NNN items.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The maximum number of items that can be produced using 444 eggs and 444 chocolates is 444, so the answer is −1-1−1.
Example case 2: In the optimal solution, the friends should order 222 omelettes, 111 chocolate milkshake and 111 chocolate cake, with cost 1⋅2+2⋅1+3⋅1=71 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 71⋅2+2⋅1+3⋅1=7.
Example case 3: In the optimal solution, the friends should order 444 chocolate cakes, with cost 1⋅4=41 \cdot 4 = 41⋅4=4.
